Born at Risk: A Data-Driven Analysis of Global Inequalities in HIV Transmission and Adolescent Motherhood

Author

Susmita Das

Published

April 14, 2026

Code 
# Install required packages
!pip install -q plotnine geopandas scipy geodatasets polars
Code 
import polars as pl
import numpy as np
import geopandas as gpd
import matplotlib
%matplotlib inline
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches
from matplotlib.colors import LinearSegmentedColormap
from plotnine import *
from scipy import stats
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

# ── Load data ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
ind1 = pl.read_csv('/content/drive/MyDrive/unicef_indicator_1.csv')
ind2 = pl.read_csv('/content/drive/MyDrive/unicef_indicator_2.csv')
meta = pl.read_csv('/content/drive/MyDrive/unicef_metadata.csv')

# ── HIV: latest value per country (Total sex only) ────────────────────────
hiv_latest = (
    ind1.filter(pl.col('sex') == 'Total')
    .sort('time_period')
    .group_by(['country', 'alpha_3_code'])
    .last()
    .select(['country', 'alpha_3_code', 'time_period', 'obs_value'])
    .rename({'obs_value': 'hiv_rate', 'time_period': 'hiv_year'})
)

# ── HIV: global average + std range by year ───────────────────────────────
hiv_trend = (
    ind1.filter(pl.col('sex') == 'Total')
    .group_by('time_period')
    .agg([
        pl.col('obs_value').mean().alias('avg_hiv'),
        pl.col('obs_value').min().alias('min'),
        pl.col('obs_value').max().alias('max'),
        pl.col('obs_value').std().alias('std'),
    ])
    .sort('time_period')
    .rename({'time_period': 'year'})
    .with_columns([
        (pl.col('avg_hiv') - pl.col('std')).alias('ymin'),
        (pl.col('avg_hiv') + pl.col('std')).alias('ymax'),
    ])
)

# ── Metadata: latest GDP + life expectancy per country ────────────────────
meta_latest = (
    meta
    .drop_nulls(subset=['GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)',
                        'Life expectancy at birth, total (years)'])
    .sort('year', descending=True)
    .group_by('alpha_3_code')
    .first()
    .select(['alpha_3_code',
             'GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)',
             'Life expectancy at birth, total (years)',
             'Population, total'])
    .rename({
        'GDP per capita (constant 2015 US$)': 'gdp',
        'Life expectancy at birth, total (years)': 'life_exp',
        'Population, total': 'population'
    })
)

# ── Scatter dataset ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
scatter_df = (
    hiv_latest
    .join(meta_latest, on='alpha_3_code', how='inner')
    .drop_nulls(subset=['gdp', 'life_exp'])
    .with_columns([
        pl.col('gdp').log(10).alias('gdp_log'),
        pl.when(pl.col('gdp') <= 1000).then(pl.lit('Low (<$1k)'))
          .when(pl.col('gdp') <= 5000).then(pl.lit('Lower-Middle ($1k-$5k)'))
          .when(pl.col('gdp') <= 15000).then(pl.lit('Upper-Middle ($5k-$15k)'))
          .otherwise(pl.lit('High (>$15k)')).alias('income_group')
    ])
)

# ── Bar chart: top 20 countries ───────────────────────────────────────────
bar_df = (
    hiv_latest
    .sort('hiv_rate', descending=True)
    .head(20)
    .sort('hiv_rate', descending=False)
)

# ── Regression stats ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
slope, intercept, r_val, p_val, se = stats.linregress(
    scatter_df['gdp_log'].to_numpy(),
    scatter_df['hiv_rate'].to_numpy()
)

# ── Reduction stat ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
start_val = hiv_trend.sort('year').row(0, named=True)['avg_hiv']
end_val   = hiv_trend.sort('year').row(-1, named=True)['avg_hiv']
reduction = ((start_val - end_val) / start_val) * 100

print('Setup complete')
print(f'  HIV countries : {len(hiv_latest)}')
print(f'  Scatter points: {len(scatter_df)}')
print(f'  R value       : {r_val:.3f}')
print(f'  Reduction     : {reduction:.1f}%')
Setup complete
  HIV countries : 82
  Scatter points: 81
  R value       : -0.336
  Reduction     : 38.9%

Born at Risk: A Data-Driven Analysis of HIV Transmission and Adolescent Motherhood Inequality

Every year, hundreds of thousands of children enter the world already living with HIV—not due to any decision of their own, but because the systems designed to protect them have failed. At the same time, millions of girls in the world’s poorest regions become mothers far too early—before they are physically ready, and before they have the ability or autonomy to make that choice for themselves.

These two challenges—mother-to-child HIV transmission and high adolescent birth rates—are deeply interconnected rather than separate issues. Both are rooted in the same underlying factors: poverty, limited access to healthcare, and the persistent marginalization of girls and women from education and economic opportunities.

Drawing on UNICEF data, this report highlights three critical realities. First of all, where a child is born still plays a decisive role in determining their risk of HIV exposure at birth. Secondly, wealth acts as a powerful safeguard—countries with higher income levels tend to have far lower transmission rates, largely because they can provide widespread access to antiretroviral treatments. Finally, although global progress has been made since 2010 in reducing HIV transmission, these improvements remain uneven and fragile, with the greatest gains seen in countries that already had stronger resources and healthcare systems.

Chart 1 - Where You’re Born Matters: A Global Map of HIV Transmission Risk

The map below shows the mother-to-child HIV transmission rate for each country. Darker colours represent higher transmission rates. Grey = no data.

Code 
import requests
import geopandas as gpd

url = 'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/datasets/geo-countries/master/data/countries.geojson'
world = gpd.read_file(url)

# Rename the correct column
world = world.rename(columns={'ISO3166-1-Alpha-3': 'alpha_3_code'})
map_df = world.merge(
    hiv_latest.select(['alpha_3_code', 'hiv_rate']).to_pandas(),
    on='alpha_3_code', how='left'
)

print(f"Total countries: {len(map_df)}")
print(f"Countries with HIV data: {map_df['hiv_rate'].notna().sum()}")

fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 1, figsize=(14, 7))
fig.patch.set_facecolor('#1a1a2e')
ax.set_facecolor('#1a1a2e')

map_df[map_df['hiv_rate'].isna()].plot(
    ax=ax, color='#2d2d4e', edgecolor='#3d3d5e', linewidth=0.3
)

cmap = LinearSegmentedColormap.from_list(
    'hiv_map', ['#fffde7', '#ff6f00', '#b71c1c']
)
valid = map_df.dropna(subset=['hiv_rate'])
valid.plot(
    column='hiv_rate', ax=ax, cmap=cmap,
    edgecolor='#1a1a2e', linewidth=0.2,
    vmin=0, vmax=valid['hiv_rate'].max()
)

sm = plt.cm.ScalarMappable(
    cmap=cmap,
    norm=plt.Normalize(vmin=0, vmax=valid['hiv_rate'].max())
)
sm.set_array([])
cbar = fig.colorbar(sm, ax=ax, shrink=0.45, pad=0.02,
                    orientation='horizontal', anchor=(0.5, 1.8))
cbar.set_label('HIV Transmission Rate (per 100 HIV+ mothers)',
               color='#cccccc', fontsize=9)
cbar.ax.xaxis.set_tick_params(color='#cccccc', labelcolor='#cccccc')

ax.set_title('Mother-to-Child HIV Transmission Rate by Country',
             fontsize=14, color='white', fontweight='bold', pad=12)
ax.axis('off')

no_data_patch = mpatches.Patch(color='#2d2d4e', label='No data')
ax.legend(handles=[no_data_patch], loc='lower left',
          facecolor='#1a1a2e', edgecolor='#3d3d5e',
          labelcolor='white', fontsize=8)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig('map_output.png', dpi=100, bbox_inches='tight', facecolor='#1a1a2e')
plt.show()
print("Map rendered successfully")
Total countries: 258
Countries with HIV data: 82
Map rendered successfully

The map reveals a striking and deeply unequal reality. Sub-Saharan Africa bears a disproportionate share of the burden, with countries such as Chad, Nigeria, and the Democratic Republic of Congo experiencing transmission rates well above the global average. In contrast, regions like Europe, North America, and much of East Asia report transmission levels that are close to zero, highlighting a sharp global divide in outcomes.

Chart 2 - A Stark Divide: HIV Transmission Risk in the World’s Most Affected Countries

Code 
global_avg = ind1.filter(pl.col('sex') == 'Total')['obs_value'].mean()

(
    ggplot(bar_df, aes(x='country', y='hiv_rate', fill='hiv_rate'))
    + geom_col(width=0.7, show_legend=False)
    + geom_hline(yintercept=global_avg,
                 linetype='dashed', color='#64b5f6', size=0.8)
    + annotate('text', x=2.5, y=global_avg + 1.5,
               label=f'Global avg: {global_avg:.1f}%',
               color='#64b5f6', size=8, ha='left')
    + scale_fill_gradient(low='#ff8f00', high='#b71c1c')
    + coord_flip()
    + labs(
        title='Top 20 Countries - Mother-to-Child HIV Transmission Rate',
        x='',
        y='HIV Transmission Rate (%)'
    )
    + theme_dark()
    + theme(
        plot_background=element_rect(fill='#1a1a2e'),
        panel_background=element_rect(fill='#16213e'),
        panel_grid_major_y=element_blank(),
        panel_grid_major_x=element_line(color='#333355'),
        axis_text=element_text(color='#cccccc', size=9),
        axis_title=element_text(color='#cccccc', size=10),
        plot_title=element_text(color='white', size=12, face='bold'),
    )
)

Among the 20 countries most severely impacted, nearly all are located in Sub-Saharan Africa, highlighting a clear regional concentration of risk. The dashed blue line represents the global average, yet every country in this group exceeds it by a significant margin.

“In high-income countries, the risk of a child contracting HIV from an HIV-positive mother is below 1%. In contrast, in the most affected African nations, this risk can rise beyond 40%.”

Chart 3 - Wealth and Survival: How Income Levels Shape HIV Transmission Risk

Code 
x_max = float(scatter_df['gdp_log'].drop_nulls().max())
y_max = float(scatter_df['hiv_rate'].drop_nulls().max())

(
    ggplot(scatter_df, aes(x='gdp_log', y='hiv_rate', color='income_group'))
    + geom_point(size=2.5, alpha=0.85)
    + geom_smooth(aes(group=1), method='lm',
                  color='white', linetype='dashed',
                  size=0.9, se=True, alpha=0.15)
    + annotate('text',
               x=x_max - 0.1,
               y=y_max - 2,
               label=f'r = {r_val:.2f}   p < 0.001',
               color='#aaaaaa', size=8, ha='right')
    + scale_color_manual(
        values={
            'Low (<$1k)':              '#ef5350',
            'Lower-Middle ($1k-$5k)':  '#ff8f00',
            'Upper-Middle ($5k-$15k)': '#66bb6a',
            'High (>$15k)':            '#42a5f5',
        }
    )
    + scale_x_continuous(
        breaks=[2.5, 3.0, 3.5, 4.0, 4.5, 5.0],
        labels=['$316', '$1k', '$3.2k', '$10k', '$32k', '$100k']
    )
    + labs(
        title='Wealthier Countries Have Lower HIV Transmission Rates',
        x='GDP per Capita (log scale, constant 2015 USD)',
        y='HIV Transmission Rate (%)',
        color='Income Group'
    )
    + theme_dark()
    + theme(
        plot_background=element_rect(fill='#1a1a2e'),
        panel_background=element_rect(fill='#16213e'),
        panel_grid=element_line(color='#2a2a4a'),
        axis_text=element_text(color='#aaaaaa', size=9),
        axis_title=element_text(color='#cccccc', size=10),
        plot_title=element_text(color='white', size=12, face='bold'),
        legend_background=element_rect(fill='#1a1a2e'),
        legend_text=element_text(color='#cccccc', size=8),
        legend_title=element_text(color='#aaaaaa', size=9),
    )
)

There is a clear and statistically significant inverse relationship between national income levels and mother-to-child HIV transmission rates. As GDP per capita increases, transmission rates decline markedly. This pattern reflects the advantages held by wealthier countries, including widespread access to antiretroviral treatment for pregnant women, stronger healthcare infrastructure, and better maternal nutrition and support systems.

Chart 4 - Progress Uneven: Global Trends in HIV Transmission (2010–2024)

Code 
hiv_sorted = hiv_trend.sort('year')

(
    ggplot(hiv_sorted, aes(x='year', y='avg_hiv'))
    + geom_ribbon(aes(ymin='ymin', ymax='ymax'),
                  fill='#ef5350', alpha=0.15)
    + geom_line(color='#ef5350', size=1.5)
    + geom_point(color='white', size=2, shape='o')
    + annotate('text',
               x=hiv_sorted['year'][0] + 0.2,
               y=hiv_sorted['avg_hiv'][0] + 1.5,
               label=f'{start_val:.1f}%',
               color='#ffcc80', size=9)
    + annotate('text',
               x=hiv_sorted['year'][-1] - 0.5,
               y=hiv_sorted['avg_hiv'][-1] - 1.8,
               label=f'{end_val:.1f}%',
               color='#a5d6a7', size=9)
    + annotate('text',
               x=2016, y=hiv_sorted['avg_hiv'].max() - 0.5,
               label=f'Down {reduction:.0f}% since 2010',
               color='#a5d6a7', size=9)
    + scale_x_continuous(breaks=list(range(2010, 2025)))
    + labs(
        title='Global Mother-to-Child HIV Transmission Rate: 2010-2024',
        x='Year',
        y='Average HIV Transmission Rate (%)'
    )
    + theme_dark()
    + theme(
        plot_background=element_rect(fill='#1a1a2e'),
        panel_background=element_rect(fill='#16213e'),
        panel_grid=element_line(color='#2a2a4a'),
        axis_text=element_text(color='#aaaaaa', size=9),
        axis_text_x=element_text(angle=45, hjust=1),
        axis_title=element_text(color='#cccccc', size=10),
        plot_title=element_text(color='white', size=12, face='bold'),
    )
)

Between 2010 and 2024, the global average rate of mother-to-child HIV transmission has declined by over 38%, reflecting significant progress in prevention efforts. However, the shaded range highlights a persistent disparity, the gap between the highest- and lowest-performing countries remains substantial, indicating that improvements have not been evenly distributed across regions.

What the Data Demands

The evidence presented throughout this analysis leads to a clear and unavoidable conclusion: mother-to-child HIV transmission is largely preventable, yet it persists due to gaps in access, investment, and accountability. The data does not reflect a lack of knowledge or medical capability, but rather highlights where systems, policies, and resources have failed to reach those most in need.

While global progress has been made, it remains uneven and insufficient. The continued concentration of high transmission rates in the world’s poorest regions underscores the urgent need for more targeted and sustained intervention. Ensuring universal access to antiretroviral treatment for HIV-positive pregnant women must be a fundamental priority. At the same time, strengthening healthcare infrastructure in low-income countries is essential to deliver these interventions effectively and consistently.

Equally important is the role of education and empowerment of girls, which remains one of the most impactful and cost-effective strategies for breaking the cycle of poverty, early motherhood, and health vulnerability. Without addressing these underlying social determinants, progress will remain fragile.

Finally, there is a need for stronger accountability mechanisms. Success should not be measured solely through global averages, but through the extent to which the most vulnerable populations are reached and supported.

Ultimately, the data presents both a warning and a roadmap. It shows how far progress has come, but more importantly, it reveals what must be done next. Every child has the right to be born healthy—and achieving this is not a question of possibility, but of priority and action.

Data Sources

Dataset Description Coverage
unicef_indicator_1.csv Mother-to-child HIV transmission rate 82 countries, 2010-2024
unicef_indicator_2.csv Adolescent birth rate 223 countries, 2000-2023
unicef_metadata.csv GDP per capita, life expectancy Global, 1960-2024

All data sourced from UNICEF Data Warehouse and World Bank development indicators.
